function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Based on the provided text, here are Amazon's reportable segments and how the company reports its capital expenditures:

### **Reportable Segments**
Amazon organizes its operations into **three** reportable segments:
1. **North America:** Primarily consists of retail sales of consumer products (including third-party sellers), advertising, and subscription services through North America-focused online and physical stores. This includes export sales from these specific stores.
2. **International:** Primarily consists of retail sales, advertising, and subscription services through internationally-focused online stores. It includes export sales from these international stores (even to U.S., Mexico, and Canada customers) but excludes export sales originating from North America-focused stores.
3. **Amazon Web Services (AWS):** Consists of global sales of compute, storage, database, and other technology services to start-ups, enterprises, government agencies, and academic institutions. 

*(Note: The company's Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM) is the CEO, who reviews consolidated net sales, operating expenses, and operating income by these three segments on a quarterly basis.)*

### **Reporting of Capital Expenditures**
Amazon reports and discusses capital expenditures primarily through the lens of cash flow, strategic investment purposes, and lease vs. purchase decisions:

* **Cash Capital Expenditures (Investing Activities):** CapEx is reported as "cash capital expenditures" within the cash provided by/used in investing activities section. For example, the text notes cash capital expenditures were **$77.7 billion in 2024** and **$128.3 billion in 2025**.
* **Categorization by Strategic Purpose:** Rather than just listing asset types, Amazon reports the *purpose* of its CapEx, noting that it primarily reflects:
  * **Technology Infrastructure:** The majority of which is invested to support AWS business growth.
  * **Fulfillment Network:** Investments in additional capacity to support its fulfillment operations.
* **Purchase vs. Lease Decisions:** Amazon explicitly ties its capital expenditures to its strategic decisions to either *purchase or lease* property and equipment (including leasehold improvements). This is further reflected in their XBRL dimensional tagging, where the company tracks and reports `AssetsHeldUnderFinanceLeases` and `AssetsUnderFinancingObligations` specifically broken down by the **AWS** segment and the combined **North America and International** segments.
* **Free Cash Flow Metric:** Capital expenditures are reported as a primary driver of Amazon's non-GAAP "free cash flow" metric. The company states that its financial focus is on long-term, sustainable growth in free cash flow, which is heavily influenced by how efficiently it manages cash capital expenditures alongside operating income and working capital.

Based on the provided text, Amazon discloses the following regarding its satellite network investment, launch services deposits, and the criteria for commercial viability:

**1. Current Accounting Treatment (Expensing)**
*   Amazon currently expenses the majority of the costs associated with developing its satellite network for global broadband service. This includes production, launch, payroll costs, and launch services deposits upon launch. 
*   These expenses are recorded under **"Technology and infrastructure"** costs, which are generally expensed as incurred.

**2. Satellite Launch Services Deposits (Balance Sheet Placement)**
*   Prior to launch or commercial viability, satellite network launch services deposits are included in **"Other assets"** and **"Prepaid expenses and other current assets"** on the consolidated balance sheets. 
*   *(Note: Prepaid expenses and other current assets, which include these deposits, were $6.3 billion and $6.9 billion as of December 31, 2024, and December 31, 2025, respectively).*

**3. Commercial Viability (Capitalization & Reclassification)**
*   Amazon has a specific trigger for changing the accounting treatment of these assets. The company states: *"We will reclassify the satellite network launch service deposits to construction-in-progress included within 'Property and equipment, net' on our consolidated balance sheet **once the service achieves commercial viability, including sales to customers**."*
*   Similarly, the company notes that it will **capitalize** certain development costs associated with the satellite network once this same commercial viability threshold (including sales to customers) is met.

**4. Broader Investment & Forward-Looking Context**
*   The satellite network is highlighted as a key initiative alongside autonomous vehicles and AI/machine learning, reflecting Amazon's broader investments in technology and infrastructure to enhance customer experience and operate at scale.
*   In its Q1 2026 guidance, Amazon specifically calls out approximately **$1 billion of higher year-over-year "Amazon Leo" costs** (its low Earth orbit satellite network) as it scales the service in 2026.

## SpaceX Connectivity Capex vs. Amazon Project Kuiper: Comparative Deployment-Stage Assessment

### 1. SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex (from S-1)

SpaceX's S-1 discloses the following Connectivity segment capital expenditures (which primarily reflect Starlink constellation build-out and ground infrastructure):

| Period | Connectivity Capex |
|---|---|
| FY2023 | $2,455 million |
| FY2024 | $3,498 million |
| FY2025 | $4,178 million |
| Q1 2026 | $1,332 million (annualized ~$5,328 million) |

Cumulative Connectivity capex over FY2023–Q1 2026 totals approximately **$11.5 billion** across roughly 2.25 years. SpaceX's Starlink constellation as of March 31, 2026 has approximately **9,600 satellites in orbit**, serves **10.3 million subscribers** across 164 markets, and generated **$11.4 billion in FY2025 Connectivity revenue**. The constellation is mature, revenue-generating, and now deploying next-generation V2 Mini satellites while planning V3 deployment via Starship in H2 2026.

### 2. Amazon Project Kuiper Commitment

Amazon has publicly committed **more than $10 billion** to Project Kuiper (announced alongside FCC authorization in July 2020). Industry analysts (e.g., Bank of America, Morgan Stanley) have estimated total program costs at **$16.5–$20 billion**. As of mid-2025, Amazon had launched only **~54 Kuiper satellites** (two batches of 27 via ULA Atlas V in April and June 2025) out of a planned **3,236-satellite constellation**. Amazon faces an FCC mandate to deploy at least **1,618 satellites (50%) by July 30, 2026**, a deadline it appears unlikely to meet at current launch cadence. The service has not yet achieved commercial viability—Amazon has rebranded it as "Amazon Leo" and guided for approximately **$1 billion of higher year-over-year Kuiper/Leo costs** in 2026 as it scales.

### 3. What the Figures Suggest About Relative Deployment Stage

The comparison reveals a **massive asymmetry in constellation maturity**:

- **SpaceX is in a mature, scaling phase.** Its $4.2 billion FY2025 Connectivity capex represents *sustaining and expanding* an already-operational constellation of ~9,600 satellites serving 10.3 million paying subscribers. SpaceX is spending to grow capacity (next-gen V3 satellites, mobile constellation, Starship-enabled mass launches), not to achieve initial deployment. The capex is being funded in part by $11.4 billion in segment revenue—the constellation is at least partially self-funding.

- **Amazon is in a pre-commercial, early-deployment phase.** Its $10 billion+ commitment is a *forward-looking, multi-year program budget* for a constellation that has launched fewer than 60 satellites as of mid-2025. Amazon is currently **expensing** most Kuiper costs (production, launch, payroll) under "Technology and infrastructure" operating expenses rather than capitalizing them, because the service has not yet achieved commercial viability. The $10 billion floor represents total planned investment over the life of the program, not annual run-rate spending.

In short: SpaceX's annual Connectivity capex (~$4.2B in FY2025) is roughly **40% of Amazon's entire multi-year $10B+ commitment floor**, yet SpaceX is spending that amount *every year* to maintain and grow a constellation that is already 175× larger in orbit (~9,600 vs. ~54 satellites). This underscores that Amazon is years behind SpaceX in deployment and has not yet begun generating meaningful satellite broadband revenue.

### 4. Analytical Limitation: Segment-Level Disclosure vs. Program-Level Commitment

The core analytical limitation is an **apples-to-oranges comparison** arising from three structural mismatches:

1. **Temporal mismatch:** SpaceX's Connectivity capex is an *actual, incurred, annual figure* reported for specific fiscal periods. Amazon's $10 billion is a *cumulative, multi-year forward commitment* announced in 2020, with no disclosed annual breakdown or actual spend-to-date figure. Comparing a single year of SpaceX capex ($4.2B) against a multi-year Amazon commitment ($10B+) understates Amazon's annual run-rate and overstates the gap if Amazon is in a heavy-deployment year.

2. **Scope mismatch:** SpaceX's Connectivity segment capex encompasses *all* connectivity-related capital spending—satellite manufacturing, launches, ground station infrastructure, user terminal production, and network operations for a mature, revenue-generating business. Amazon's $10 billion is a *program-specific* estimate for Kuiper constellation build-out only, and does not include broader AWS ground infrastructure or integration costs that may also support Kuiper.

3. **Accounting treatment mismatch:** SpaceX capitalizes its Connectivity segment expenditures (reported as capex in segment disclosures). Amazon currently **expenses** most Kuiper costs as incurred under "Technology and infrastructure" operating expenses because the service has not achieved commercial viability. This means Amazon's Kuiper spending may not appear as capital expenditures at all in its 10-K, making direct capex-to-capex comparison impossible until Amazon begins capitalizing.

### 5. Specific Amazon 10-K Line Items to Triangulate Kuiper Investment

An analyst seeking to triangulate Amazon's actual Kuiper spending should consult the following specific line items in Amazon's 10-K:

**Primary line item: "Total net additions to property and equipment" by segment** (in the Segment Reporting footnote). This table breaks down capex by North America, International, AWS, and Corporate segments. However, Kuiper is **not separately disclosed** in any segment—its costs are likely embedded in the **"Corporate"** category ($2.3 billion in FY2025) or potentially within **AWS** ($96.5 billion in FY2025) given Kuiper's integration with AWS infrastructure. Amazon does not break out Kuiper as a sub-segment or separate reporting unit.

**Secondary line items for triangulation:**

- **"Other assets"** on the consolidated balance sheet ($82.1B in 2024; $122.6B in 2025): This line item explicitly includes **"satellite network launch services deposits"**—pre-launch deposits for Kuiper satellites. Changes in this balance year-over-year can signal the pace of Kuiper investment, though it is commingled with equity investments, content, intangibles, and deferred tax assets.

- **"Prepaid expenses and other current assets"** ($6.3B in 2024; $6.9B in 2025): Also includes satellite launch services deposits that are expected to be utilized within 12 months.

- **"Technology and infrastructure" operating expense** ($88.5B in 2024; $108.5B in 2025): This is where Amazon currently **expenses** Kuiper production, launch, and payroll costs. The $20 billion year-over-year increase is driven primarily by AWS/AI infrastructure, but Kuiper costs are embedded here and not separately broken out.

- **"Construction in progress"** within Property and Equipment ($46.6B in 2024; $71.8B in 2025): Amazon has disclosed that it will **reclassify satellite launch deposits to CIP** once Kuiper achieves commercial viability. A future spike in CIP could signal the transition to commercial operations.

The fundamental challenge is that Amazon's 10-K provides **no standalone Kuiper line item** in either its income statement or segment reporting. An analyst must rely on management commentary (e.g., the Q1 2026 guidance citing ~$1 billion in incremental "Amazon Leo" costs), balance sheet changes in "Other assets," and the gap between total "Technology and infrastructure" expense growth and known AWS/AI drivers to estimate Kuiper's actual spend rate.
